headline: EPF Flexible Account Withdrawals Reach RM14.79B by 30 June 2025
context: MOF reported in a written Parliament answer that RM14.79 billion had been withdrawn from EPF Akaun Fleksibel by 4.63 million members as of 30 June 2025, about 35% of EPF members under 55, since the facility launched in May 2024. EPF later said usage reached 37% by October 2025.

cards.big: RM14.79 billion withdrawn from EPF Akaun Fleksibel by 30 June 2025. 4.63 million members had made at least one withdrawal.
cards.sub: EPF later found most use was for essentials. Akaun Fleksibel launched May 2024 as a permanent flexible-withdrawal facility.

cards.big: Post-pandemic EPF withdrawal facilities have reduced the compound growth base for millions of retirement accounts unless replenished.
cards.sub: The 2020-2022 special withdrawals (i-Lestari, i-Sinar, i-Citra) plus Akaun Fleksibel collectively pulled tens of billions from long-term savings.

cards.big: Akaun Fleksibel was framed as an emergency facility, but its design allows withdrawal at any time, for any reason, with no eligibility test.
cards.sub: Members earning below RM3,000 made up 51.1% of withdrawers, broadly mirroring EPF's overall membership composition rather than disproportionate low-income use.

cards.big: Around 36% of active formal EPF members met the RM240,000 basic-savings benchmark in 2024. The new RIA framework targets RM390,000 as the new basic floor.
cards.sub: Akaun Fleksibel withdrawals reduce balances available for retirement unless replenished. EPF itself frames the facility as a trade-off.

cards.big: Calling it 'flexible savings' does not change the math. Ringgit withdrawn today and not replenished compound into a larger gap at retirement.
Like eating your seed corn in a drought. It solves today's hunger but cuts into next year's harvest.
cards.sub: Withdrawn ringgit, unless replenished, no longer earn EPF dividends until retirement.

cards.big: Akaun Fleksibel helps short-term liquidity; EPF data show most use was for essentials. Its scale also signals household-finance pressure that policy should address upstream.
